chapters.title: Shielding the powerful and burying the truth
chapters.text:
In 2017, Ronan Farrow stumbled into an investigation into the crimes of Harvey Weinstein. He turned out to be the only journalist brave enough to expose the full scope of what Weinstein had done.

Weinstein was a famous producer, both respected and feared across Hollywood and the press. He made stars, and he was the godfather figure for many actors and actresses. Yet behind the curtain, the maker was also a destroyer.

Weinstein stood out in the movie industry like nobody else. With over 300 Oscar nominations to his name, he was the dream producer for every actor. He started his first company, Miramax, with his brother Bob, and Disney bought it in the early 1990s. When that chapter ended, he launched the Weinstein Company.

Predators target victims who can be intimidated. Their actions need to be publicized and condemned.

Beneath the image of a successful producer, he was a sly predator. His actions went far past mistreating business partners and actors. He subjected them to sexual assault. The gap between his public persona and his real behavior was staggering. Even with his many humanitarian causes and his central role in Hillary Clinton's campaign, he betrayed the trust of the people around him. He raised large sums for Clinton while wearing a mask of warmth, which made the concealed truth even harder to see.

Many journalists shied away from negative stories about powerful figures like Weinstein. It was not until October 2016, when sexual allegations against Bill Cosby and Donald Trump made the news, that the press woke up to the need to use its tools in the service of justice.

This summary walks through the untold stories that led to Weinstein's downfall and highlights the way the media tried to protect influential people from the law. Let us pull back the curtain together.

chapters.title: The part the press played
chapters.text:
Ronan Farrow and Rich McHugh worked together on several investigations and found that while some reporters avoided the topic of sexual assault, others did not. Among the brave ones was Billy Bush, the anchor of the celebrity news program Access Hollywood. The information Farrow and McHugh gathered from Bush proved vital to their investigation into Weinstein and other predators.

The media industry, however, was a world of its own. The secrets it held seemed endless. Every time one came to light, more seemed to pop up in its place.

Theologian Dietrich Bonhoeffer once wrote that silence in the face of evil is itself evil.

Weinstein used his wide network inside the media to hide his crimes. Any victim who dared to accuse him of sexual abuse was either paid off or labeled unstable by the press. Other journalists had tried to report the Weinstein story in the past but stopped, because they could not afford to risk their careers.

Many actresses chose not to share their stories, but Rose McGowan stood apart. Farrow first met her in 2010 and realized that she openly loathed the patriarchal structures in Hollywood.

Sexual violence is often silenced by society, which leaves victims without support. Open, honest conversations can ease the stigma.

Other journalists, including Seth Freedman, an English writer for the Guardian, tried to reach McGowan, who appeared to be under pressure. After talking with her, Farrow interviewed Dennis Rice, a seasoned marketing executive, who revealed that Miramax had paid off women who were harassed or sexually abused by Weinstein. Rice also said he had personally witnessed Weinstein's inappropriate behavior, and that the women had been warned their careers would be over if they spoke out.

As the list of complaints about Weinstein grew, well-known names like Rose McGowan and Asia Argento came forward. In her on-camera testimony, McGowan bravely recounted being raped and being paid 100,000 dollars for her silence.

While Farrow tried to gather more evidence to back up McGowan's account, some people warned him to drop the story. Donna Gigliotti, the producer of Shakespeare in Love, told him he had a lot to lose if he kept going. That only strengthened Farrow's resolve to uncover the truth.

Farrow's drive to expose sexual predators traces back to the fact that his own sister was a victim of sexual abuse.

chapters.title: The case against Weinstein needed proof
chapters.text:
Noah Oppenheim, the newly appointed president of NBC News, was briefed by Farrow on what McGowan and other Miramax executives had shared about Weinstein. Oppenheim insisted that more evidence was needed to justify opening an investigation. So Farrow set out to find more victims willing to be interviewed.

A safe environment is necessary for victims of violence to open up.

For all the crimes that came to light, the producer faced only one more public accusation in the press. The report came from a Miss Italy finalist named Ambra Battilana Gutierrez in 2015. Farrow reached her through her lawyer, and she agreed to meet.

Her story showed how money and power can make problems disappear. She described how Weinstein had made inappropriate advances toward her during a meeting in his Tribeca office. When she reported the incident to the police, they suggested she wear a wire during her next appointment to draw out a confession.

The plan did not go as hoped. Although officers arrested him at the hotel, no charges were filed after news spread calling Gutierrez a prostitute, in a move that likely came from Weinstein's influence. On April 20, 2015, she received a 1 million dollar settlement and signed away her right to speak publicly or press charges against Weinstein.

The 18 page agreement, unknown to them, could not erase every trace of Weinstein's assault. Gutierrez had saved three audio files on her laptop, in which Weinstein admitted to sexual assault, promised career advancement in exchange for sexual favors, and named other actresses he had helped.

After a few weeks, she was ready to let NBC's legal department listen to the audio, even though she had not decided whether to submit it as evidence. Following advice from his sister Dylan, Farrow contacted Lisa Bloom, a lawyer who was passionate about defending survivors of sexual violence. Bloom offered names of attorneys with experience in nondisclosure agreement cases, and even offered to connect Farrow directly with Weinstein and his lawyer David Boies, which strengthened the investigation.

chapters.title: Tracking the helpers while pushing the investigation forward
chapters.text:
Farrow became even more convinced of the allegations after speaking with Ken Auletta, a former writer at The New Yorker, who had started an investigation into Weinstein back in 2002 but had to give it up because he could not find enough evidence.

Evil always finds supporters, which is why strong laws are needed to leave no room for dodging accountability.

He then met Ben Wallace, the New York Magazine writer behind the most recent attempt at the Weinstein story. Wallace warned him that Weinstein always seemed to find out about investigations that touched him. Wallace also hinted that some of the people he worked with might be double agents passing information back to Weinstein. He pointed to a woman named Anna and a former Guardian writer, Seth Freedman, as the most suspicious. Unable to move the story forward, Wallace gave up the Weinstein story in January 2017.

Farrow decided he would not let the story die the same way. He realized that he could obtain a recording of Weinstein admitting to his crimes from Gutierrez by having her play the audio while he recorded it fresh. That approach cut the risk and avoided the need to transfer files. The trouble was, Farrow began receiving threats from an unknown Instagram account. This time, the threats included a picture of a gun.

Through Ben Wallace, Farrow met Weinstein's former assistant, Emily Nestor, who agreed to go on air. Her claim that harassment was normal in Weinstein's corporate culture was backed up by other accounts. She also spoke about the activities of Irwin Reiter, who had worked with Weinstein for three decades. Nestor told her story clearly: she was 25 when she started at the Weinstein Company in 2014, and Weinstein wasted no time getting her phone number. He pushed her to meet him for drinks, but she declined and offered an early coffee. Weinstein accepted and chose the Peninsula Hotel as the venue.

Over coffee, he offered her a career boost in exchange for a romantic relationship, saying other famous actresses had agreed to such arrangements. She confided in a friend, who quickly reported the incident to the company's human resources office on her behalf. She also mentioned that Weinstein's assistants were aware of his behavior with young women and sometimes stayed at the start of those encounters to create a false sense of comfort for the victims. The alarming revelation showed how far Weinstein's misconduct reached, and how many people around him were quietly enabling it.

chapters.title: Black Cube and the other pieces falling into place
chapters.text:
Weinstein noticed the storm building against him, so he turned to the intelligence agency known as Black Cube for help. They signed a confidentiality agreement that set off aggressive operations aimed at stopping the press from publishing accusations against him.

Farrow knew the story had to be published, and soon. He gathered the evidence he had collected, a list of sources, transcripts of conversations, the audio from Gutierrez, and a write-up of patterns and settlements, and stored it all in a safe deposit box at the Bank of America. He felt it was essential to protect those documents, given the danger he might be in.

As time went on, more people agreed to share their experiences on camera. Katrina Wolfe, a former assistant who had become an executive, said two female employees had accused Weinstein of sexual assault and settled out of court.

Farrow went through Ken Auletta's files and saw how many pieces of the Weinstein case fit together. The files detailed how he had harassed an employee named Zelda Perkins, since she had to work close to him, and then shifted that attention to her assistant Rowena Chui. Chui and Perkins resigned and received a 250,000 pound settlement, split between them, in exchange for their silence.

The script of the Weinstein story was straightforward. It included the audio tape credited to Gutierrez, McGowan's recorded testimony, and Nestor's interview with Irwin Reiter's messages as evidence. Former employees' contributions and the uncovered settlements and negotiations tied back to Weinstein's account were also part of the package.

Always seek the truth and fight for justice, no matter how many obstacles stand in the way.

After the script was reviewed by Susan Weiner, the general counsel of NBC News, and deemed satisfactory, it still did not sit well with Noah Oppenheim, who was an executive producer of NBC's Today Show at the time. He insisted they wait for another lawyer's verdict before running the story.

The heads of NBC Universal then decided to put the story through a legal review. The decision took an unexpected turn when NBC's general counsel, Kim Harris, told them the script on Weinstein could expose them to an illegal interference claim. Harris asked them to work on a revised script to lower the risk of a lawsuit. As a result, Farrow halted the case and canceled previously scheduled interviews at Oppenheim's insistence. The investigation was suddenly stopped, leaving Farrow to rethink his strategy and face the obstacles ahead.

chapters.title: Lose the job, or step away from the case?
chapters.text:
Despite the obstacles and warnings, Farrow soon had a new lead, a woman named Ally Canosa. Having worked with Weinstein since 2010, Canosa confirmed that she was willing to share how she had endured repeated sexual assaults from him.

In the middle of the chaos, former New York state governor George Pataki quickly informed Harvey Weinstein that Farrow was still on the case. Weinstein reached out to NBC officials, who reassured him that everything was under control.

In August 2018, Richard Greenberg, the interim head of NBC's investigative unit, told Farrow that the revised script had been approved. Still, Oppenheim and Andy had not agreed to air it. Farrow was accused of being obsessed with the Weinstein story because of his sister's history of sexual abuse. They also worried about a possible conflict of interest. For that reason, they still refused to air it.

It was clear that NBC was not going to let the Weinstein story see the light of day. To drive the point home, they seized all the materials that Farrow and McHugh had gathered from different sources.

Luckily, McHugh had a backup file he had made from the original document, and he quickly put it in a safe deposit box. He asked Auletta if The New Yorker would run the story instead. The next day, Auletta introduced Farrow to the editor of The New Yorker, David Remnick.

The chances of The New Yorker accepting the script and airing the story were slim. Still, after much deliberation, Farrow was allowed to work with a young editor named Deirdre Foley-Mendelssohn.

On September 11, 2018, Oppenheim told Farrow that the network had no room in the budget for him anymore and could not offer him a steady position. During his time on the investigation, he was also warned not to mention his time at NBC.

Know your rights as a citizen of your country, and do not let anyone manipulate you or your rights.

At that point, Farrow began receiving threats from Weinstein's legal team. They warned him not to use any of the interviews, to hand over his work products, and said they would sue him for defamation if he used any of the content. He refused to back down and kept the investigation alive.

For context, Weinstein had his assistants keep track of the women he harassed in a file named FOH, short for friends of Harvey.

chapters.title: The story finally ran
chapters.text:
Farrow persuaded the award-winning actress Mira Sorvino, the daughter of the actor Paul Sorvino, to talk about her experience with Weinstein. She described how Weinstein had tried to massage her shoulders and make her uncomfortable. He had also tried to force his way into her room at night. Rosanna Arquette, an actress and producer, confirmed the picture and shared the belief that Mira's career suffered because she had refused him.

Soon the Italian actress Asia Argento also recounted how she had been invited to a party thrown by Miramax, and the head of Miramax Italy had led her to Weinstein's hotel room, where Weinstein forced himself on her. When Farrow heard that Sophie Dix, an English actress, had shared a similar Weinstein hotel experience, as confirmed by her informed friends and family, he knew the story was now untouchable.

Still, the road was not smooth. Every day brought more dead ends as people backed out for fear of Weinstein. But Argento's partner, Anthony Bourdain, encouraged Farrow to press on and keep working the story, even when nothing seemed to be going right.

Fame can be a powerful tool, either to lift people up or to manipulate and exploit them.

As Foley-Mendelssohn and Remnick reviewed the scripts, The New Yorker's effort on the story grew. The investigation was led by two determined reporters, Megan Twohey and Jodi Kantor, who approached their work with relentless dedication and made sure every detail was carefully checked and handled.

As fact-checkers at The New Yorker began contacting various sources, Weinstein fired back by sending his first legal letter to the publication. Despite the legal risks, Remnick reassured Farrow of their support and promised him legal protection in the face of any lawsuit.

The Hollywood Reporter and Variety then ran stories about a possible battle between Weinstein and The New Yorker over a potentially explosive story, which made Weinstein and his team nervous.

When Weinstein received a call from Farrow asking for comment, he told Farrow he was barking up the wrong tree and could not save everyone. He added that any future questions should go to a member of his legal team, Lisa Bloom.

More people kept coming forward with their stories as the script was reviewed. Among them was Lucia Evans, a marketing consultant who accused Weinstein of forcing her to perform oral sex in 2004 after he tricked her into a fake meeting.

On October 10, 2018, Foley-Mendelssohn circulated the final edit, and the story ran. People began reaching out to Farrow for comment as the story spread across the internet.

chapters.title: A chain reaction across the media
chapters.text:
Seth Freedman, a former stockbroker turned writer, reached out to Farrow and said he was interested in the story. During their talk, Freedman told Farrow about the existence of the Black Cube.

In an email Farrow received from an anonymous source, he discovered that over 1.3 million dollars had been spent on funding the Black Cube, and that the law firm Boies Schiller, which represented the New York Times, was aware of the arrangement. The names of the collaborators who helped Weinstein were also released, which shook the media a second time.

Follow and join initiatives that fight against sexual crimes. Support their work and tell your friends about them.

Sources inside and around American Media Inc. (AMI) insisted that Weinstein was not the only figure who deserved to be exposed. The lawyer Carol Heller wrote to Farrow that an American model named Karen McDougal had signed away her right to speak about her affair with Donald Trump. At Farrow's urging, she began to share her story. She had dated Trump for nine months and ended it because she felt guilty about the affair. AMI employees admitted that this was how many stories disappeared, because there was no evidence left to back them up. Reports about people like Arnold Schwarzenegger, Sylvester Stallone, Tiger Woods, and other celebrities were shelved because AMI bought them and buried them in a method called Catch and Kill.

NBC also faced sexual assault accusations against Matt Lauer, the co-host of NBC's Today Show. Lauer was eventually fired after a female colleague made an allegation against him. Others were also found guilty of sexual abuse in the workplace, as many women began telling their stories.

The Weinstein investigation set off a chain of reactions across the media world. More criminals were brought to justice. NBC's most prominent political analyst, Mark Halperin, was fired after five women came forward to say he had been harassing them. Matt Zimmerman, an executive at the Today Show, also lost his job because of sexual relations with two young workers. Many began to accuse NBC of having an unsafe work culture.

In the end, the courage of women cannot be stamped out. And stories, the big ones, the true ones, can be caught but never killed, as Ronan Farrow has written.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
This summary has a clear message. No matter how long evil has held the upper hand, the truth is strong enough to bring it into the light. No amount of status, money, or connections should justify violence. There is no room for mistreating others and then pretending everything is fine.

Ronan Farrow and his investigation show that silencing crimes only breeds more violence. But victims have to be brave enough to tell their stories. Recognize that threats from perpetrators and agreements to stay silent come from their fear of being held accountable. With the support of the people around them, the press, and professionals, all victims have a chance to win and to restore justice.

The courage of the women affected by Harvey Weinstein exposed all the underhanded moves of these powerful men, because the truth cannot be killed. These women did not just find freedom through the investigation. They also found justice.

No one has the right to take control of other people's lives and make them doubt their own abilities through dishonesty and humiliation. And so, even though we must remember that many predators like Weinstein still exist, we have to do our part to make sure they face the consequences of their actions.

Try this. Protect your organization and family by creating a safe environment where everyone can freely share their thoughts and concerns without fear of judgment. Organize events that encourage employees to speak up against sexual harassment and clearly outline the consequences of such behavior.

Made it to the end?
